entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: MY WORKMANSHIP IN THE LORD.
entries.body: The really great thing was that the Corinthian Christians were Paul’s workmanship in the Lord. Even Judas was an apostle and saw Christ, but because he did not have the work of an apostle, these things were of no benefit to him. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 21.2

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: AN APOSTLE TO THE CORINTHIANS.
entries.body: Those Jewish believers who nevertheless continued to observe the law of Moses denied that Paul was an apostle because he taught that it was no longer necessary to be circumcised or to observe the sabbath. Even the other apostles thought that he was teaching something different because of this, and they denied that he was an apostle. But to the Corinthians Paul was an apostle, because they had seen the signs of God’s power in him. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: HE BECAME EVERYONE’S APOSTLE.
entries.body: Once again, Paul makes his point by concession, for the whole world had him as its apostle. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 21.2

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PAUL’S DEFENSE.
entries.body: Here Paul begins to develop the argument which he set out above, namely: All things are lawful for me, but I will not be enslaved by anything (6:12). {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodoret of Cyr
entries.title: TO THOSE WHO WOULD EXAMINE HIM.
entries.body: Paul says that if anyone wants to examine his works, let him look at the Corinthians, for they are a sufficient witness to his labors. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Commentary on the First Epistle to the Corinthians 219

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FOOD AND DRINK.
entries.body: This is what Paul meant when he said that all things were lawful to him. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: ACCOMPANIED BY A WIFE.
entries.body: The apostles concentrated on undistracted preaching and took their wives around as Christian sisters rather than as spouses, to be their fellow ministers to the women of the household, so that the gospel would reach them without causing scandal. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE MINISTRY OF SISTER WOMEN.
entries.body: When his identity has been established, he shows that the privileges granted to the other apostles are his also, namely, exemption from manual labor and livelihood in recompense for his preaching as the Lord appointed. This is stated most clearly according to the verses where St. Paul argues explicitly that faithful women, possessing the goods of this world, went along with the apostles and ministered to them from their own supplies that the servants of God might lack none of those commodities which constitute the necessities of life. . . . Certain persons, not understanding this passage, have interpreted it as wife. The obscurity of the Greek word deceived them, since, in Greek, the same word is used for wife and woman. Yet the apostle has placed the words in such a way that people should not be deceived, since he says not merely a woman but a sister woman, and not to take in marriage but to take about. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE RIGHT TO BE ACCOMPANIED.
entries.body: To what right does he refer unless it be to the one which the Lord gave to those whom he sent to preach the kingdom of heaven when he said: Eat what they have; for the laborer deserves his wages?{fn:0} He offered himself as an exponent of this privilege. Very faithful women attended to the necessities of his life at their own expense. {fn:1}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: THE RIGHT TO REFRAIN FROM WORKING.
entries.body: Paul means by this that he and Barnabas do have this right, but they do not want to exercise it. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: LIVE BY THE GOSPEL.
entries.body: The Lord directed those who preach the gospel to live by the gospel, that is, to maintain at the expense of the faithful that life for which food and clothing are essential. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: A SOLDIER AGAINST THE DEMONIC.
entries.body: The apostleship was much more dangerous than being a soldier. For their warfare was not just with men but with demons as well. The apostles were both soldiers and husbandmen and shepherds, not of the earth, nor of irrational animals, nor in such wars as are perceived by the senses, but of rational souls and in battle array with the demons. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 21.4

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE PROPRIETY OF RECEIVING SUPPORT.
entries.body: The church has its own soldiers and its own provincial officers . . . its vineyard and its planters, its flock and its shepherds. . . . Thus some are rightly fed and clothed at the expense of the charitable rich. They accept nothing for their own necessities except from those who sell their goods. They are not to be judged and condemned by the more perfect members of Christ who furnish their own needs with their own hands—a higher virtue which the apostle strongly commends.{fn:0} They in turn ought not to condemn as Christians of lower grade those from whose resources they are supplied. . . . The servants of God who live by selling the honest works of their own hands could, with much less impropriety, condemn those from whom they receive nothing than could those others who are unable to work with their hands because of some bodily weakness yet who condemn the very ones at whose expense they live. {fn:1}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WRITTEN IN THE LAW.
entries.body: Paul indicates here that his position corresponds to the teaching of Scripture and that his refusal to accept any payment from the Corinthians was with good reason. {fn:0}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: GOD’S CARE FOR THE APOSTLES.
entries.body: God’s care was not only for the oxen{fn:0} but moreso for the apostles, for whose sake he uttered these words. {fn:1}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: AN OX TREADING GRAIN.
entries.body: Why does Paul mention this, when he could have used the example of the priests?{fn:0} The reason is that he wanted to prove his case beyond any shadow of doubt. If God cares about oxen, how much more will he care about the labor of teachers? {fn:1}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 21.5

entries.author: St. Fulgentius of Ruspe
entries.title: OXEN AND MEN.
entries.body: These animals accomplish their life and purpose in this world according to the incomprehensible will of the Creator. They render no account of their deeds because they are not rational. Is God concerned about oxen? Human beings, however, because they have been made rational, will render an account to God for themselves and for all the things which they have received for use in this present life. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: REASONING BY ANALOGY.
entries.body: The whole of Scripture applies to us by way of analogy. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: SPIRITUAL GOOD AND MATERIAL BENEFIT.
entries.body: Paul points out that those who contribute to their teachers receive more than they give. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 21.6

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: MANUAL LABOR JUSTLY SUBSTITUTED.
entries.body: Paul emphasizes the fact that his fellow apostles were not transgressing in any way when they did not engage in manual labor to provide the necessities of life, but, as the Lord directed, living on the gospel, they accepted, without offering payment, bodily nourishment from those to whom they in turn furnished spiritual nourishment without demanding payment. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WE ENDURE ANYTHING.
entries.body: Paul does not exercise his rights because they might be an obstacle to the gospel. That left him free to argue that he was not one of the false apostles. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: FOOD FROM THE TEMPLE.
entries.body: The case of the apostles was much stronger than that of the priests. The priesthood was an honor, but the apostles were exposed to dangers, slaughters and violent deaths. In saying We have seen spiritual good among you he points to the storms, the dangers, the snares, the unspeakable evils endured in preaching. But Paul was unwilling to despise the things of the old law or to exalt what belonged to him. He even provided his own possessions. He reckoned their value not from the dangers but from the greatness of God’s gift. He did not say if we have exposed ourselves to danger but if we have sown spiritual things among you. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 22.1

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: LIVING FROM ONE’S LABOR.
entries.body: It was not by the law of Moses that God followed the practice of the Gentiles, but natural reason itself decrees that a person should live from his labor. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE RIGHT OF SUPPORT.
entries.body: The apostle says this [here and in 2 Timothy 2:3-6] so that Timothy might understand that what he took from those for whom he was, as it were, fighting, and whom he was cultivating as a vine, or feeding as a flock, was not a sign of begging but an acknowledgment of a right. {fn:0}

pericopes.pericopeTitle: PAUL’S COMMISSION TO PREACH THE GOSPEL

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THE CLARITY OF THE TEXT EXCEEDS ITS EXPLANATIONS.
entries.body: The Lord directed that those who preach the gospel should have their living from the gospel. But I for my part have appealed to none of these rights. . . . What is clearer than this? What is more definite? My only fear is that, when I discuss the passage in an attempt to explain it, I may obscure that which is of itself patent and forceful. For, they who do not understand these words, or pretend that they do not understand them, understand mine much less. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: LOOK TO THE SAINTS FOR APPLICATIONS.
entries.body: For the laborer deserves his hire.{fn:0} He showed that this practice{fn:1} was permitted, though not commanded, lest perhaps a disciple who took some compensation for his personal needs from those to whom he was preaching might think he was doing wrong. That it was more commendable to omit this practice is shown clearly in the life of the apostle . . . [who] declared: Nevertheless I have not used this right. . . . He possessed the right, but he did not bind his followers by a command. Since we are, then, unable to comprehend many passages, we gather from the deeds of the saints how to understand those passages which may easily be misinterpreted if reference is not made to the example set by the saints. {fn:2}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: WILLINGNESS TO DIE.
entries.body: It is better that I die than that they ravish and plunder some of my brothers and by sly verbal deception lead captive the little children and sucklings in Christ. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: I WOULD RATHER DIE.
entries.body: Paul says he would rather die because he knew it would be better from the standpoint of his future salvation. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: PREACHING THE GOSPEL.
entries.body: The servant sent by the Lord does what he has to do even if he is not willing, because if he does not do it he will suffer for it. Moses preached to Pharaoh even though he did not want to,{fn:0} and Jonah was forced to preach to the Ninevites.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: THE DISPENSATION OF THE WORD.
entries.body: What then shall I do, to whom the dispensation of the Word is committed? Although I am an unprofitable servant,{fn:0} I have, nevertheless, received from the Lord the commission to distribute the measure of wheat to the master’s servants.{fn:1} {fn:2}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: IN CHARITY OF SPIRIT.
entries.body: Surely it is better to merit a reward than to serve as a steward. Let us not be bound by the yoke of slavery, but let us serve in charity of spirit. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: DISPENSING AND PARTAKING OF SALVATION.
entries.body: Up to now I am so far restored in that glory that I confess I am ignorant not only how near I come to it but even whether I shall come to it at all. It is true I am a dispenser of eternal salvation along with my other innumerable fellow servants. For if I do this thing willingly, I have a reward. To be a dispenser of that salvation by word and sacrament is not at all the same as to be a partaker of it. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: REWARD.
entries.body: What can equal preaching? For it makes men vie even with the angels themselves. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 22.3

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: FREE FROM ALL, SLAVE TO ALL.
entries.body: The fact that he is completely free makes Paul the exemplary apostle. For it is possible to be free of immorality but a slave to anger, to be free of greed but a slave to boasting, to be free of one sin but a slave to another. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: WANTING ONLY THEIR SALVATION.
entries.body: Paul is free from all human claims because he preached the gospel without getting any praise for it and never wanted anything from anyone, except their salvation. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Alexandria
entries.title: LOSING A PART TO GAIN ALL.
entries.body: So also the blessed Paul became all things to all men, not in order that he might gain some sort of advantage but that, with the loss of a part, he might gain all. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: NOT A PRETENSE.
entries.body: Did Paul merely pretend to be all things to all men, in the way that flatterers do? No. He was a man of God and a doctor of the spirit who could diagnose every pain, and with great diligence he tended them and sympathized with them all. We all have something or other in common with everyone. This empathy is what Paul embodied in dealing with each particular person. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: NOT BEING UNDER THE LAW.
entries.body: Paul did not become a Jew in reality but only in appearance. How could it have been otherwise, since he was so determined to convert them and deliver them from their predicament? {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 22.5

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: NOT A DECEPTION.
entries.body: Paul was not pretending to be what he is not but showing compassion. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: THINKING SYMPATHETICALLY.
entries.body: A person who nurses a sick man becomes, in a sense, sick himself, not by pretending to have a fever but by thinking sympathetically how he would like to be treated if he were sick himself. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: UNDER THE LAW OF CHRIST.
entries.body: To be under the law of Christ is to be under the law of God, because everything which is of Christ is of God. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.body: Paul states, somewhat surprisingly, that he is under the law of Christ, lest anyone think that he is under the law of Moses. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: PUTTING ONESELF IN THE OTHER’S PLACE.
entries.body: He did this by compassion, not by lying. For each one becomes like him whom he wants to help when such great mercy prevails as that each one would wish for himself if he were in the same misery. And so he becomes like the other—not by deceiving him but by putting himself in the other’s place. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: NOT A LIE.
entries.body: This is correctly interpreted to mean that he, not by lying but by sympathy, brought it about that he enabled their conversion by his own great love which made it seem as though he himself were afflicted with that evil of which he wished to heal them. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Cyril of Jerusalem
entries.title: CHRIST AS PATTERN OF EMPATHY.
entries.body: Everywhere the Savior becomes all things to all men. To the hungry, bread; to the thirsty, water; to the dead, resurrection; to the sick, a physician; to sinners, redemption. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: PAUL THE IMITATOR OF CHRIST.
entries.body: He who did not think it robbery to be equal with God took the nature of a slave. He became all things to all men to bring salvation to all. Paul, an imitator of him, lived as if outside the law while remaining accountable to the law. He spent his life for the advantage of those he wished to win. He willingly became weak for the weak in order to strengthen them. He ran the race to overtake them. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: ALL THINGS TO ALL PEOPLE.
entries.body: Paul became weak by abstaining from things which would scandalize the weak. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: TO SAVE SOME.
entries.body: Paul became all things to all men, not in the expectation that he would win everybody but that he might save at least some. It was not possible for all the seed to be saved, but neither could it be that all of it should perish. Someone as ardently zealous as Paul was sure to have some success at least. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 22.5

entries.author: Origen of Alexandria
entries.title: FOR THE SAKE OF THE GOSPEL.
entries.body: Only someone as mature in faith as the apostle Paul could say this. A sinner could never talk in this way. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: CONTENT TO SHARE.
entries.body: Do you perceive Paul’s humility, how in the recompense of rewards he places himself as one of the many, even though he had exceeded all the others in his labors? It is obvious that his reward would be greater also, but he does not attempt to claim the first prize. On the contrary, he is content simply to share with the others the crowns which are laid up for them. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 22.5

entries.author: Pseudo-Clement
entries.title: NOT ALL ARE CROWNED.
entries.body: So then, my brothers, let us strive, knowing that the contest is close at hand and that many make voyages for corruptible contests, but not all are crowned—only those who have labored much and striven well. 2 {fn:0}

entries.author: Tertullian
entries.title: THE HARSH REGIMEN OF THE ATHLETE.
entries.body: Your master, Jesus Christ, has anointed you with his Spirit and has brought you to this training ground. He determined long before the day of the contest to take you from a softer way of life to a harsher regimen, that your strength may increase. Athletes are set apart for more rigid training to apply themselves to the building up of their physical strength. They are kept from lavish living, from more tempting dishes, from more pleasurable drinks. They are urged on, they are subjected to tortuous toils, they are worn out. The more strenuously they have exerted themselves, the greater is their hope of victory. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Gregory of Nyssa
entries.title: GREATNESS OF SOUL.
entries.body: As far as you extend your efforts in behalf of piety, so far will the greatness of your soul extend through efforts and toils toward what the Lord urges us. {fn:0}
entries.citation: On the Christian Mode of Life

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: THE DILIGENCE REQUIRED TO WIN.
entries.body: Paul says this not because he thinks that only one person will be saved but because he wants to point out how much diligence is required in order to succeed. It is not enough merely to believe and then contend in any which way. Unless we have run our race in such a way as to be blameless and to come near to the prize, it will not benefit us. Even if you think you are perfect in your knowledge you have still not obtained everything, so you must continue to run in order to obtain the prize. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 23.1

entries.author: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite
entries.title: THE SACRED CONTEST.
entries.body: The initiate is summoned to the sacred contests, which, with Christ as his trainer, he must undertake. For it is Christ who, as God, arranges the match, as sage lays down the rules, as beauty is a worthy prize for the victors, and more divinely as goodness is present with the athletes, defending their freedom and guaranteeing their victory over the forces of death and destruction. And so the initiate will quite gladly hurl himself into what he knows to be divine contests, and he will follow and scrupulously observe the wise rules of the game. . . . He will follow the divine tracks established by the goodness of the first of athletes. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: WILLING AND RUNNING BY GRACE.
entries.body: It is not from the one who wills nor from the one that runs but from God who has mercy that we obtain what we hope for and reach what we desire. Esau was unwilling and did not run. Had he been willing and had he run, he would have obtained the help of God who by calling him would have given him the power both to will and to run. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Clement of Alexandria
entries.title: ATHLETES OF SELF-CONTROL.
entries.body: No effort, no crown! Today there are people who place the widow above the virgin in terms of self-control, because the widow has rejected a pleasure which she once enjoyed. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Cyprian of Carthage
entries.title: THE COMBAT.
entries.body: This combat was foretold by the prophets, engaged in by the Lord and carried on by the apostles. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: THE ATHLETE.
entries.body: You are an athlete. Come to grips with your opponent, not with your head but with your arms. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Basil the Great
entries.title: THE RULES.
entries.body: No one is crowned except he strive lawfully. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: REFRESHED BY HOPE.
entries.body: When we enter upon the way of the Lord, let us fast from the vanity of this present life and refresh ourselves with the hope of the future life, not focusing our heart on things here but feasting it on things above. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: FIGHTING EFFECTIVELY.
entries.body: Paul means that he fights not merely with his words but with his deeds. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: INTO THE ARENA.
entries.body: Like an athlete he comes last into the arena. He lifts his eyes to heaven. . . . He sees that his whole task awaits him. . . . He chastises his body so that it will not defeat him in the contest. He anoints it with the oil of mercy. He practices daily exhibitions of virtue. He smears himself with dust. He runs with assurance to the goal of the course. He aims his blows, he darts his arms, but not at empty spaces. . . . Earth is man’s training ground, heaven his crown. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Ambrose of Milan
entries.title: PARRYING BLOWS.
entries.body: Like a good athlete, Paul knew how to parry the blows of the opposing powers and even to strike them as they advanced to the attack. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Jerome
entries.title: THIS WORLD AND THE NEXT.
entries.body: So run in this world as to obtain in the next. {fn:0}

entries.author: Theodore of Mopsuestia
entries.title: THE POWERS OF THE AIR.
entries.body: The air here refers to the powers of evil. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Pauline Commentary from the Greek Church

entries.author: St. Jerome
entries.title: LET THE SOUL COMMAND THE BODY.
entries.body: Let our soul be in command and our body in subjection. Then Christ will come immediately and make his dwelling with us. {fn:0}

entries.author: Ambrosiaster
entries.title: POMMEL THE BODY.
entries.body: To pommel the body is to fast and to avoid any kind of luxury. Paul shows that he disciplines his own body so that he will not miss out on the reward about which he preaches to others. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: BEHAVIORAL CHANGE REQUIRED.
entries.body: Here Paul is implying that the Corinthians are subject to the lusts of the belly and abandon themselves to it, fulfilling their own greediness under a pretense of perfection. If Paul, who had taught so many, was afraid of being rejected at the end, what can we say? Mere belief is not enough; we must behave in a way which is blameless if we hope to inherit salvation. {fn:0}
entries.citation: Homilies on the Epistles of Paul to the Corinthians 23.2

entries.author: St. John Chrysostom
entries.title: PAUL’S NEED FOR SELF-DISCIPLINE.
entries.body: If Paul—a man of such caliber and stature, who traversed the whole world like a winged creation, who proved superior to bodily necessities and was privileged to hear those secret words that no one else to this day has heard—if he wrote these words, I punish my body and bring it into subjection lest while preaching to others I myself become disqualified, if then that man, the object of so great favor, despite such conspicuous prowess felt the need to pommel his body, bring it into subjection, submit it to the authority of the soul and place its impulses under the virtue of the soul . . . what then would we say, deprived as we are of these virtues and with nothing to show in addition to this beyond deep indifference? After all, this war admits of no truce, does it? It has no set time for the assault, does it? {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Augustine of Hippo
entries.title: DISTINGUISHING THE JUSTIFIED SELF FROM THE EVIL CONSEQUENCES OF FREE ACTS.
entries.body: Paul chastises what is of him and not what is himself. For what is of him is one thing, what is himself is another. He chastises what is of him so that he, being just, may bring about the death of bodily wantonness. {fn:0}

footnotes.text: FC 35:85. He does not chastise the new man made just in faith but what is of or from the old man of sin.

entries.author: Cassiodorus
entries.title: THE UNEVEN CONTEST.
entries.body: Free us, Lover of men, from the danger which Paul mentions, that while preaching to others I may myself be found false. You truly know how weak we are. You recognize the nature of the foe who oppresses us. In our uneven contest and our mortal weakness we seek you, for the glory redounds to your majesty if the roaring lion is overcome by the feeble sheep. {fn:0}

entries.author: St. Irenaeus of Lyons
entries.body: This able wrestler [Paul], exhorts us to the struggle for immortality, that we may be crowned, and may deem the crown precious, namely, what is acquired by our struggle, but which does not encircle us of its own accord. And the harder we strive, the more valuable it is; the more valuable it is, so much the more should we esteem it. And indeed things that come spontaneously are not esteemed as highly, as those that are reached by much anxious care.
